fact: February 2 is exactly 33 days away from both New Year's Day and the first day of March - it sits perfectly balanced between them like a calendar seesaw!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many animals are still deep in hibernation, but their heart rates start speeding up slightly as their bodies sense spring is coming in about 6 weeks.
The sun rises about 2 minutes earlier each day around February 2 in most places, which means you're gaining almost a quarter-hour of daylight each week!
Tree sap starts flowing again around this time in maple trees across cold regions, even though there's still snow on the ground - it's like the trees are waking up and stretching!
February 2 falls smack in the middle of Aquarius season, making it one of the most 'Aquarius-y' days of the year for anyone who follows zodiac signs.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, February 2 is peak summer - the hottest, sweatiest, most ice-cream-melting time of their entire year!
This date is exactly 46 days before the spring equinox in the north, which ancient people considered a magical halfway point between winter's darkest day and spring's perfect balance.
Polar bears in the Arctic are typically giving birth to tiny, hairless cubs around February 2 - the babies are only about the size of hamsters and completely helpless!
The moon phase on February 2 changes every year, but when it's a full moon on this date, it creates some of the most brilliant winter night skies because the snow reflects all that moonlight.
In many cold places, February 2 marks when the soil is frozen at its deepest point underground - sometimes rock-hard for 3 feet down or more!
Certain flowers like snowdrops and winter aconites often start poking through frozen ground around this time, making them some of the bravest plants on Earth.
The jet stream - those fast rivers of air high in the sky - tends to be at its wildest and most unpredictable around early February, causing crazy weather swings.
